title: Building a Manure Heap for Your Smallholding
subtitle: Siting, layering, turning, and legal requirements before use
excerpt: A well-managed manure heap turns waste into valuable compost for your land. This tutorial covers where to site it legally, how to build and turn it, and when it is safe to spread. NVZ rules apply to many smallholdings.
sourceNotes: DEFRA Nitrate Vulnerable Zones guidance; Environment Agency Farming: managing your land to prevent water pollution; The Nitrate Pollution Prevention Regulations 2015; RB209 Fertiliser Manual (AHDB). Synthesised from official UK government and AHDB publications.

glossaryTerms.definition: Nitrate Vulnerable Zone. A designated area of land where stricter rules apply to fertiliser and manure spreading, to protect water quality.
A mixture of animal dung, urine, and bedding material such as straw. When composted it becomes a valuable soil improver.
Any channel through which water flows, including rivers, streams, ditches, and drains. Pollution of a watercourse from a manure heap is a criminal offence.
The liquid that drains from a manure or compost heap. It contains high levels of nutrients and can pollute water if not managed.

content.text: Legal requirements for siting
Before you build a heap, check your legal position. Manure must not be stored where
leachate
can run into a
watercourse
. The Environment Agency takes water pollution seriously. Fines for polluting a watercourse can be substantial.
Site your heap at least 10 metres from any watercourse, ditch, or drain. Choose ground that slopes away from water. Avoid low-lying or boggy areas where leachate can pool and flow.
Check whether your land is in a
NVZ
. In an NVZ, extra rules apply to how long manure can be stored and when it can be spread. Use the government Magic Map tool online to check your postcode.
Choosing a site
At least 10 metres from any watercourse, ditch, or drain
At least 50 metres from any borehole or spring used for drinking water
Away from prevailing wind direction relative to your house and any neighbours
Accessible by wheelbarrow or tractor for loading and unloading
On firm, ideally compacted ground or a concrete pad to contain leachate
What goes in the heap
Farmyard manure
is the main ingredient. This means dung mixed with bedding straw or wood shavings. Wet manure from slurry pens needs a separate slurry store, which has different regulations.

You can add vegetable and garden waste to the heap. Do not add kitchen scraps, meat, or processed food. Adding the wrong material can attract pests and may be illegal under animal by-product regulations.
Building the heap

A three-bay system works well on a smallholding. Bay one is for fresh material. Bay two is for material that is actively composting. Bay three holds finished material ready to use. Build bays from pallets or concrete blocks. Gaps between boards allow airflow.
If you have only one species or a small number of animals, a single heap works fine. Just work in layers and mark the date you started each new section.
Layer the heap as you add to it. Aim for roughly equal parts dung and bedding. Pack each layer loosely to allow air in. Very dense layers slow the composting process.
Covering the heap

Cover the heap with a breathable sheet or tarpaulin. This prevents leachate from rain running off the site. It also keeps heat in, which speeds decomposition. Use weights or bungee cords to hold the cover down in wind.
Turning schedule

Turn the heap every four to six weeks. Fork material from the outside to the inside. The outside cools quickly and decomposes slowly without turning. Moving it inward exposes it to the heat at the core.
After turning, the heap should heat up again within a few days. This heat kills weed seeds and pathogens. If the heap stays cold after turning, it may be too dry. Add water if needed.
Maturation time

Most livestock manure needs at least six months to mature before spreading on vegetable ground. Pig manure should be composted for at least 12 months. Poultry manure is hot and high in nitrogen. Compost it for at least six months and use it sparingly.
Finished compost looks dark and crumbly. It smells earthy, not of ammonia or dung. If it still smells sharp, give it more time.
NVZ spreading restrictions

If you are in an NVZ, you cannot spread manure during a closed period. For most land in England the closed period runs from 1 October to 31 January for grass, and 1 August to 31 January for arable ground. Check the current rules for your county and soil type. Spreading during a closed period is a criminal offence.
Even outside an NVZ, never spread on waterlogged, flooded, or frozen ground. Nutrients run straight off into drains and watercourses.
